evals.prompt: I'm working in appsmith-v2 and adding some polish to custom-domain support. When a user's custom domain finishes DNS propagation, we need to kick off Google Search Console verification, wait for it to come back, and then flip the domain to active. My plan is one function in the domain service — finalize_domain(domain_id) — that runs the DNS check, makes the GSC verification call, waits for the result, and updates the domain row to active at the end. Before I write it: does that approach fit how we build things in this repo, and what should I actually do?
evals.expected_output: A guidance-lookup answer grounded in the appsmith-v2 engineering principles. It should identify this as the Deployment & Domains component, recommend modeling the flow as a committing, resumable state machine (Principle 16) with explicit persisted intermediate states rather than one blocking finalize_domain function, recommend doing the slow external GSC work asynchronously off the request path (Principle 19), and recommend bounding the GSC verification retries with an explicit terminal state (Principle 21). It should advise against the proposed single-function approach and cite the relevant principle numbers.
expectations: Identifies the work as belonging to the Deployment & Domains area of the platform
Recommends modeling the flow as a resumable state machine that commits each transition (Principle 16), not one blocking function
Recommends the domain move through explicit persisted intermediate states (e.g. pending to verifying to active) that can resume after a crash
Recommends running the slow external GSC verification work asynchronously off the request path rather than waiting inside a single call (Principle 19)
Recommends bounding the verification retries with backoff and an explicit terminal state (Principle 21)
Explicitly advises against the user's proposed single blocking finalize_domain function as written
Grounds the advice in the appsmith-v2 principles catalogue and cites specific principle numbers rather than giving only generic best practice

evals.prompt: I'm reviewing a teammate's PR in appsmith-v2 before I approve it. The attached file is a new endpoint they added at backend/app/routes/export_routes.py. Can you check whether it's consistent with how we build backend code in this repo, and flag anything that should change before it merges?
evals.expected_output: A conformance review report. It should identify the relevant components (Backend HTTP Routes, plus the service/database layer), and flag the deviations: business logic in the route handler (Principle 2), the route reaching directly into the database and models instead of going through a service (Principle 1), missing typed Pydantic request/response schemas (Principle 7), the slow export generation running inline in the request path instead of on a queue (Principle 19), and the non-idempotent create (Principle 15). Each deviation should have a concrete remediation and a severity. The report should end with a clear verdict.
expectations: Produces a structured conformance review report with a clear overall verdict
Identifies the relevant component(s): Backend HTTP Routes and the service/database layer
Flags that business logic (format validation, quota enforcement, generation) lives in the route handler, violating the thin-edges standard (Principle 2)
Flags that the route reaches directly into the database and models instead of delegating to a service, violating separation of concerns and layering (Principle 1)
Flags the missing typed Pydantic request and response schemas, with raw string params and plain dict returns (Principle 7)
Flags that the slow export generation runs inline in the request path instead of being pushed onto a queue or worker (Principle 19)
Flags that creating an export is not idempotent, so a retried POST creates duplicate records (Principle 15)
Gives a concrete, specific remediation for each flagged deviation and assigns a severity to it
Cites the appsmith-v2 principle numbers behind each finding rather than only generic code-review commentary

evals.prompt: Quick gut-check on something I'm building in appsmith-v2. The orchestrator needs to be able to produce an XML sitemap for a deployed app. My plan: add a new specialized SitemapAgent, and have the existing SEO agent call SitemapAgent directly whenever it decides a sitemap is needed. The sitemap-building itself — crawling the app's routes, formatting the XML — I'd put inside the new agent's reasoning loop. Does this shape make sense, or am I doing something off?
evals.expected_output: A guidance answer grounded in the appsmith-v2 agentic-system principles. It should flag that having the SEO agent call the new agent directly violates hub-and-spoke orchestration — specialized agents must not coordinate each other; coordination flows through the single orchestrator (Principle 38) and via tool results and persisted state (Principle 4). It should flag that deterministic sitemap-building does not belong in an agent reasoning loop and should live in a service or tool (Principles 2 and 6). It should raise skill-first extension — a new agent plus loop is the highest-power option and a skill/tool/service should be considered first (Principle 41). It should conclude the proposed shape is off and give a concrete better shape.
expectations: Flags that having the SEO agent call the new agent directly violates hub-and-spoke orchestration — specialized agents must not coordinate each other (Principle 38)
Recommends coordination flow through the orchestrator and via tool results and persisted state rather than direct agent-to-agent calls (Principle 4)
Flags that deterministic sitemap-building (crawling routes, formatting XML) should not live in an agent reasoning loop and belongs in a service or tool (Principles 2 and 6)
Raises skill-first extension — a new specialized agent plus loop is the highest-power option, and a skill, tool, or service should be considered first (Principle 41)
Concludes that the proposed shape is off and gives a concrete recommended alternative shape
Grounds the critique in the appsmith-v2 principles catalogue and cites specific principle numbers
